title: The People Protecting Yesterday Will Never See Tomorrow Coming
excerpt: I got redirected at work today for something small.
body:
I got redirected at work today for something small.

I was in the middle of putting product away. Moving fast. Trying to get things done.



Somewhere in the building leadership was in meetings all day. The kind of meetings where nothing gets built, nothing gets solved, and nothing actually moves. Just a circle of titles protecting each other and calling it leadership.



I did not say anything. I just kept moving.



Because I have a 10am meeting tomorrow that none of them know about.



— -



There is a specific type of person in every large organization that I have been thinking about lately.



They have been there 15 or 20 years. They know the internal politics. They know which relationships to maintain and which corners to cut. They have mastered the art of appearing busy without producing anything that could actually be measured.



They are not bad people. They are just optimized for a world that is slowly disappearing.



Legacy management built careers on institutional knowledge and seniority. On being the person who remembered how things were done before the system changed. On relationships with other legacy managers who promoted each other in a slow rotation that looked like meritocracy from the outside.



That model worked for a long time.



It is working less and less.



— -



The world is not asking whether you have been here the longest anymore.



It is asking what you have shipped. What you have built. What you can prove.



Parker Dewey does not care how many years I have spent in a single company. They care whether I can deliver a corporate strategy project for Reynolds American or build a talent pipeline tool for United Talent Agency.



LinkedIn does not care about my title. It cares whether my content resonates with 777 members who have never heard of me before yesterday.



Google does not care about my seniority. It cares whether my articles answer questions people are actually asking.



The market has quietly shifted the evaluation criteria.



Legacy management has not noticed yet.



— -



I want to be honest about something.



Building in silence is uncomfortable in a way that nobody really talks about.



You show up every day to an environment where the people around you have no idea what you are working toward. They see the surface. The job title. The daily tasks. The small redirects about things that do not matter.



They do not see the analytics climbing. They do not see the projects landing. They do not see the meeting tomorrow that could change the trajectory of everything.



And you cannot explain it to them because the gap between where you are and where you are going is still too wide for most people to bridge in their imagination.



So you just keep moving.



You post the article at midnight. You check the analytics in the morning. You build the demo before anyone asks. You apply to the projects before the deadline. You stay on the call when you want to leave.



While the legacy meeting is still happening somewhere down the hall.



— -



Here is what I have figured out.



Legacy management is not the enemy. It is just the wrong game to be playing in 2026.



The people in that circle are not malicious. They are just optimized for a system that rewarded tenure over output. Relationships over results. Presence over proof.



That system still exists in pockets. It will exist for a while longer.



But the market is building an alternative in parallel. Platforms like Parker Dewey that evaluate you on deliverables not resumes. Content platforms that reward consistency not credentials. Credentialing bodies like the IRS that grant you authority based on passing an exam not based on who you know.



The alternative is not fully built yet.



But it is being built.



And the people who figure that out now are the ones who end up with options when the old system finally finishes collapsing under its own weight.



— -



I have a 10am meeting tomorrow.



Nobody at work knows about it.



The same person who got redirected today for something small is walking into that meeting with a full stack web application, a Python scraper pulling live data from three sources, a SQLite database, an admin editorial portal, and a handoff document ready for a nonprofit newsroom that has never seen anything like it.



Legacy management would call that overqualified.



The market calls it exactly what it was looking for.



There is a difference between the two evaluations.



Learn to care about the right one.



— -



If you are building something in silence right now while the people around you protect yesterday keep going.



The meeting is coming.



They will not see it coming until it is already done.



And by then you will already be somewhere else.



— -



Matthew Coleman is the owner of MCC Solutions LLC, a licensed financial services firm in Cranford NJ. He is currently completing corporate projects for United Talent Agency and The Richmonder through Parker Dewey while building toward full time entrepreneurship.
